title: onMetric callback exceptions are silently swallowed in production builds
description: The emitFoldedMetrics helper only logs onMetric exceptions when NODE_ENV === 'development'. In production, a throwing onMetric callback causes the exception to disappear with no trace. This means callers that accidentally throw in onMetric (e.g. due to a stale closure over a disposed object) get no signal that something went wrong. The same pattern is repeated in the 'metric' message case handler at line 376.

title: getStats() ratio is NaN/Infinity when originalBytes is 0 (zero-dimension image)
description: When width or height is 0 (or the product overflows and is guarded to 0), originalBytes is set to 0. The subsequent line computes ratio = compressedBytes / originalBytes which produces Infinity or NaN without any guard. The overflow guard at line 201 only protects the originalBytes variable but does not prevent the division. A caller receiving a NaN ratio could propagate it silently into telemetry or UI display.

title: Pre-aborted AbortSignal path in EncodeSessionImpl does not guard acquirePromise race
description: When a signal is already aborted at construction time, abortHandler() is called synchronously (line 136). This calls terminate() which sets terminated=true. However, initAcquire already checks terminated before acquiring a slot (line 105), but the abortHandler call happens AFTER the acquirePromise chain is set up (line 117-122). The abortHandler fires synchronously before the microtask for initAcquire runs, so terminated=true is set before initAcquire executes — this path appears correct. However, there is a subtlety: unlike DecodeSessionImpl which checks signal.aborted before constructing the acquirePromise chain entirely (line 88), EncodeSessionImpl always constructs the acquirePromise chain and then calls abortHandler, meaning the no-op catch attached to doneDeferred at line 100 is still needed (it is), but the asymmetric ordering between decode and encode session constructors is a latent fragility if the code is refactored.

title: probeCapabilities() discards async errors with only a console.warn — no structured error reporting
description: The probeCapabilities async IIFE catches all errors and only emits a console.warn in the catch block. This is declared intentional (keep conservative defaults), but the failure is entirely invisible in environments without a console (e.g. headless workers, SSR). If @casabio/jxl-capabilities throws due to a version mismatch or import failure, the context silently operates with degraded defaults forever with no observable signal to the caller. There is no way for callers to check whether the probe succeeded or failed.

title: firstFrame() throws a bare JxlError with empty context object when stream ends without a qualifying frame
description: If the session's frame stream ends (via end() or fail()) before any frame matching minStage is observed, firstFrame() throws JxlError('Internal', 'stream ended before requested stage', {}). The empty context object {} means sessionId is missing from the error, making it difficult to correlate the error with the originating session in logs or error-tracking systems. When the stream failed with its own error, firstFrame() will propagate that error from the for-await loop naturally, but when the stream ended cleanly without the required stage, the thrown error has no session context.

category: missing error handling on async operation
title: cancel() silently swallows acquirePromise rejection instead of reporting it
description: cancel() uses acquirePromise.catch(() => undefined) to absorb any rejection from the acquire path. If the acquire failed (e.g. scheduler shutdown), the session is already terminated via the .catch handler in the constructor, so the re-check on line 207 (if terminated) will bail. However, the explicit catch-and-ignore in cancel() discards the rejection value entirely, which means if a caller awaits cancel() after an acquire failure, they get no signal about why — they just silently succeed. This mirrors the encode-session cancel path at line 217.

title: EncodeSessionImpl constructor does not validate width/height are positive integers before use
description: The constructor accepts opts.width and opts.height and forwards them directly to startMsg without any validation. A caller passing width=0, height=-1, or a non-integer (e.g. NaN) will produce a malformed encode_start message that travels all the way to the worker before any error is surfaced. The getStats() guard at line 201 only handles post-encode overflow but does not prevent issuing the malformed start. An early guard in the constructor would surface the error at the call site with a clear ConfigError.
code_excerpt:
const startMsg: MsgEncodeStart = {
      type: "encode_start",
      sessionId: this.id,
      format: opts.format,
      width: opts.width,
      height: opts.height,
      hasAlpha: opts.hasAlpha,

title: encode metric callback exceptions are fully silenced with bare empty catch
description: The encode-session metric handler (line 262-268) uses a bare catch with no body at all. Unlike the decode-session which at least logs in dev mode, the encode-session silently eats any exception thrown by opts.onMetric. This inconsistency means encode-session metric errors are harder to diagnose than decode-session ones.

title: No structured way to check whether probeCapabilities settled before decode/encode is called
description: The probeSettled flag is set on CapabilityAwareContext but is only accessible internally. Callers have no way to await the capabilities probe settling before issuing a decode/encode, meaning early calls will always use conservative defaults (e.g. wasmSimd=false). Exposing a probeSettled() method or a readyPromise() on JxlContext would allow callers to wait for the probe and get accurate capabilities before dispatching work. This would also allow test code to assert on probe results without timing hacks.

title: Unhandled message type warning only fires in NODE_ENV=development — production message type gaps are invisible
description: The default case of handleMessage only warns in development mode. In production, a new protocol message type (e.g. added in a worker update) dispatched to an older session handler silently does nothing. This makes it impossible to detect protocol version skew in production deployments. A production-safe structured log or a metrics counter for 'unknown_message_type' would surface such gaps.

category: missing error propagation / resource leak on exception
title: toTransferableBuffer call in push() is not guarded — a detached SharedArrayBuffer throws uncaught
description: push() calls toTransferableBuffer(chunk) at line 157 after all the terminated/closed/drain checks. If chunk is a Uint8Array backed by a detached or SharedArrayBuffer, the .buffer access inside toTransferableBuffer (util.ts line 32) will throw a TypeError synchronously. This exception would propagate out of push() as an untyped Error rather than a JxlError, breaking the typed error contract promised to callers. The same issue exists in encode-session pushPixels() at line 159.
code_excerpt:
const ab = toTransferableBuffer(chunk);
    scheduler.send(this.id, { type: "decode_chunk", sessionId: this.id, chunk: ab }, [ab]);

title: probeCapabilities() has no timeout — a hung getCapabilities() import stalls probeSettled forever
description: The async probe imports @casabio/jxl-capabilities and awaits getCapabilities() with no timeout. If the dynamic import or the capability probe hangs (e.g. Service Worker intercept, slow network for a remote module, or a blocked SharedArrayBuffer SAB probe), probeSettled will never become true. Any internal code that waits on probeSettled (if added in the future) would hang indefinitely. Adding a race against a timeout would make the probe robust.
